lines.translation: Zhang Tianmings Eltern wünschen sich für ihre Kinder eine gute Ausbildung. Deshalb haben sie schon bei deren Geburt angefangen, für die Ausbildung zu sparen.
Obwohl Zhang Tianmings Eltern kein geringes Einkommen haben, bedeutet es für sie immer noch eine finanzielle Belastung, ihn und seine ältere Schwester durchs Studium zu bringen.
Zhang Tianming findet, dass er schon erwachsen ist und einen Job finden sollte, um Geld zu verdienen und seine Eltern zu entlasten. Deshalb suchte er online nach passenden Jobs.
Gerade dann kamen Lisha und Xuemei herein.
Tianming, was schaust du dir da online an?
Ich möchte einen Nebenjob annehmen, um weniger Geld von meiner Familie auszugeben.
Wird ein Nebenjob dein Studium beeinträchtigen?
Ich möchte arbeiten, um etwas Berufserfahrung zu sammeln. Wenn die Arbeitszeiten nicht zu lang sind, wird es mein Studium nicht beeinträchtigen.
Ich möchte auch einen Nebenjob annehmen, um etwas Taschengeld zu verdienen.
Hast du kein Stipendium?
Das Stipendium reicht nicht aus, um die Studiengebühren zu decken, und ich habe auch einen staatlichen Studienkredit beantragt. Xuemei, arbeiten chinesische Studenten auch nebenbei?
Früher nicht, denn damals war das Studium kostenlos. Heute verlangen die Hochschulen Studiengebühren, deshalb versuchen viele Studenten auch, Wege zu finden, um zu arbeiten und Geld zu verdienen.
Wo arbeiten chinesische Studenten normalerweise? Als Kellner in Restaurants?

Es gibt einige Studenten, die in Restaurants arbeiten, aber nicht viele. In chinesischen Städten gibt es viele Menschen vom Land, die Arbeit suchen, und Restaurants stellen sie gerne ein, weil ihre Löhne niedriger sind.
Hast du schon mal einen Nebenjob gehabt?

Ja, habe ich. Ich war Nachhilfelehrerin und habe Englisch unterrichtet. Einige meiner Kommilitonen übersetzen, andere kümmern sich um Computer, und wieder andere helfen Leuten beim Gassigehen mit Hunden oder füttern Katzen.
Es scheint, als würden viele Studenten gleichzeitig studieren und nebenbei arbeiten, um Geld zu verdienen.
Apropos Geld: Meine Mitbewohnerin hat sich heute Geld von mir geliehen. Sie sagte, ihre Mutter sei wütend geworden und würde ihr kein Geld mehr geben.
Warum?

Ihre Eltern haben ihr die Mahlzeiten für das Semester bereits bezahlt, aber sie bestellt entweder Essen zum Mitnehmen oder geht mit Kommilitonen ins Restaurant. Sie kauft auch oft impulsiv Dinge, gibt rücksichtslos Geld aus und schuldet der Bank und Kreditkartenfirmen viel Geld.
Das Taschengeld, das ihre Eltern ihr letzten Monat gegeben haben, hat sie in weniger als zehn Tagen ausgegeben und dann ihre Mutter um mehr gebeten, aber es reichte immer noch nicht.
Kein Wunder, dass ihre Mutter wütend ist. Warum denkt sie nicht daran, wie schwer es für ihre Eltern ist, Geld zu verdienen!
Ja, wir sollten nicht rücksichtslos Geld ausgeben.
Jeder weiß, dass du ein Musterkind bist! Na gut, such dir in Ruhe einen Job. Wir gehen dann mal.

example_sentence.cn: 她不是叫外卖,就是跟同学去饭馆儿,还常常乱买东西,乱花钱,欠了银行和信用卡公司很多钱。

example_sentence.translation: Sie bestellt entweder Essen zum Mitnehmen oder geht mit Kommilitonen ins Restaurant. Außerdem kauft sie oft impulsiv Dinge, gibt rücksichtslos Geld aus und schuldet der Bank und Kreditkartenfirmen viel Geld.

example.cn: 她父母把她这个学期的饭钱都交了，可是她不是叫外卖，就是跟同学去饭馆儿，还常常乱买东西，乱花钱，欠了银行和信用卡公司很多钱。
example.py: Tā fùmǔ bǎ tā zhège xuéqí de fàn qián dōu jiāole, kěshì tā bùshì jiào wàimài, jiùshì gēn tóngxué qù fànguǎn er, hái chángcháng luàn mǎi dōngxī, luàn huā qián, qiànle yínháng hé xìnyòngkǎ gōngsī hěnduō qián.
example.translation: Ihre Eltern haben ihren Essensplan für dieses Semester bereits bezahlt, aber sie bestellt entweder Essen zum Mitnehmen oder geht mit ihren Kommilitonen ins Restaurant. Außerdem kauft sie oft impulsiv Dinge, gibt rücksichtslos Geld aus und schuldet der Bank und Kreditkartenfirmen viel Geld.

example.py: shéi bù zhīdào nǐ shì fùmǔ de guāi háizi hǎo ba nǐ mànmànr zhǎo gōngzuò wǒmen zǒu le
example.translation: Jeder weiß, dass du das brave Kind deiner Eltern bist! Na gut, nimm dir Zeit bei der Jobsuche. Wir gehen jetzt.

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:存] bedeutet, etwas zur Aufbewahrung irgendwo zu lassen, wie in [zh:在银行存钱] (Geld bei einer Bank einzahlen), [zh:在超市 (chāoshì) 存包] (eine Tasche im Supermarkt abgeben) und [zh:把东西存在朋友家] (Sachen bei einem Freund lassen).

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:供] bedeutet liefern, wie in [zh:供水] (Wasser liefern). Es kann auch finanzielle Unterstützung bedeuten, wie in [zh:供孩子上学] (die Ausbildung der Kinder finanzieren) und [zh:供房子] (die Hypothek bezahlen).

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.explanation_en: Im Standardchinesisch ist das Antonym von [zh:大人] [zh:小孩(子)] oder [zh:小孩儿]. Es ist nicht [zh:小人], was eine verabscheuungswürdige Person bezeichnet. [zh:小人] ist das Antonym von [zh:君子 (jūnzi)], einer tugendhaften Person.

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: Löhne und Gehälter sind [zh:工资] beziehungsweise [zh:薪水 (xīnshui)]. Auf dem chinesischen Festland wird [zh:工资] viel häufiger verwendet als [zh:薪水].

language_notes.label: e
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:家教] ist die Kurzform von [zh:家庭教师].

language_notes.label: f
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:借钱] kann je nach Verwendung entweder Geld leihen oder Geld verleihen bedeuten: „X leiht sich Geld von Y“ ist [zh:X 跟 Y 借钱]. „X leiht Y Geld“ ist jedoch [zh:X 借钱给 Y] oder [zh:X 把钱借给 Y].

language_notes.label: g
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:不到] bedeutet „hat eine bestimmte Zahl nicht erreicht“ oder „weniger als“, wie in [zh:认识不到一个月] (sich weniger als einen Monat kennen), [zh:现在不到六点] (es ist noch nicht sechs Uhr) und [zh:机票不到一千元] (das Flugticket kostet weniger als tausend Yuan).

language_notes.label: A
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:受到] wird oft von einem abstrakten Nomen gefolgt, das im Chinesischen meist wie angegeben qualifiziert wird: [zh:受到 (大家的) 欢迎] (den Empfang aller erhalten), [zh:受到 (良好的) 教育] (eine sehr gute Ausbildung erhalten) und [zh:受到 (老师的) 影响] (den Einfluss des Lehrers erhalten). Es ist leicht, [zh:受到] mit dem ähnlich klingenden Wort [zh:收到] zu verwechseln, das in Verbindung mit konkreten Objekten verwendet wird, zum Beispiel [zh:收到一封信] (einen Brief erhalten) und [zh:收到一个礼物] (ein Geschenk erhalten). Beachten Sie den Tonunterschied: [zh:受 (shòu)] versus [zh:收 (shōu)].

language_notes.label: B
language_notes.explanation_en:
[zh:压力] ist ein Nomen, das oft in Mustern wie [zh:有压力], [zh:给…压力] oder [zh:对…的压力] vorkommt.
1 Ich habe bei der Prüfung nicht gut abgeschnitten und habe Angst, dass meine Mutter wütend wird. Ich fühle mich sehr unter Druck gesetzt.
2 Da ich dieses Mal die Aufnahmeprüfung für das Graduiertenstudium mache, setzt mein Professor mich stark unter Druck.
3 Die Wirtschaft ist dieses Jahr wirklich schlecht. Ich finde keinen Job, und das setzt mich sehr unter Druck.

language_notes.label: C
language_notes.explanation_en:
[zh:减轻] wird im Allgemeinen von einem abstrakten Nomen gefolgt, wie in [zh:减轻负担] (eine Last mindern), [zh:减轻压力] (Druck lindern) und [zh:减轻痛苦 (tòngkǔ)] (Schmerz lindern).
1 Die Schüler wünschen sich, dass der Lehrer weniger Hausaufgaben gibt, um ihre Belastung zu verringern.
2 Nachdem er einen Job gefunden hat, ist sein Druck erheblich gesunken.
3 Der Arzt versuchte viele verschiedene Wege, um seine Schmerzen zu lindern.

language_notes.label: D
language_notes.explanation_en:
In IC1 haben wir [zh:合适] als Adjektiv mit der Bedeutung „passend“ gelernt. Diese Lektion lehrt [zh:适合] mit der Bedeutung „passen zu“. [zh:适合] ist ein transitives Verb, daher muss es von einem Nomen gefolgt werden oder in einem „Schlüsselsatz“ vorkommen, in dem das Objekt auch als Subjekt des folgenden Satzteils dient.
Vergleichen Sie, wie diese beiden Wörter in (1), (2) und (3) unterschiedlich verwendet werden:
1 Diese Jeans passen dir nicht. Sie könnten deiner Mutter passen.
2 Diese Art von Möbeln eignet sich für den Heimgebrauch. Sie sind nicht geeignet, um sie in ein Büro zu stellen.
3 Dieses Studienfach passt nicht zu dir. Du solltest besser ein anderes Fach wählen.

language_notes.label: E
language_notes.explanation_en:
[zh:影响] kann ein Verb sein, wie in (1) und (2) zu sehen:
1 Nicht genug Schlaf wirkt sich auf die Gesundheit aus. [Verb]
2 Lies du nur. Ich sitze hier still und werde dich nicht stören. [Verb]
[zh:影响] kann auch ein Nomen sein und erscheint oft im Muster [zh:X对Y有影响] (X hat einen Einfluss auf Y).
3 Wenn du nicht zu diesem Treffen gehst, wird das deine Jobsuche beeinflussen. [Nomen]

language_notes.label: F
language_notes.explanation_en: [zh:取得] wird von abstrakten Nomen gefolgt, wie [zh:经验], [zh:同意], [zh:好成绩 (hǎo chéngjì)] (Erfolg, eine gute Note) und [zh:进步 (jìnbù)] (Fortschritt).

language_notes.label: G
language_notes.explanation_en:
Um ein zuvor erwähntes Thema zu vertiefen, verwenden Sie [zh:说到]:
1 Apropos Geld: Meine Mitbewohnerin hat mir heute erzählt, dass ihre Mutter wütend ist und ihr kein Geld mehr geben will.
2 Person A: Worüber denkst du nach?
Person B: Ich überlege, welche Kurse ich nächstes Semester belegen soll.
Person A: Apropos Kurse fürs nächste Semester, können wir auch Kurse außerhalb unserer Schule wählen?
Person B: Wo würdest du dann Kurse belegen?
Person A: Ich möchte Kurse an der nahegelegenen Universität belegen, wäre das in Ordnung?
Person B: Ich glaube nicht.
3 Person A: Ich habe gehört, Zhang Tianmings Cousin ist ein großartiger Basketballspieler.
Person B: Das habe ich auch gehört. Apropos Basketball, sollen wir dieses Wochenende Basketball spielen gehen?

language_notes.label: H
language_notes.focus_text: 不是 X, 就是 Y
language_notes.explanation_en:
Dieses Muster wird oft verwendet, um zwei vorhersehbare Szenarien zu beschreiben:
1 Sie bestellt entweder Essen zum Mitnehmen oder geht mit ihren Kommilitonen ins Restaurant.
2 Es ist jetzt wirklich schwierig, eine Wohnung zu mieten. Entweder ist der Preis zu hoch, oder die Wohnung ist schlecht, es ist sehr mühsam.
3 Diejenigen, die gestern Abend zur Party kamen, waren entweder Kommilitonen oder Freunde, er kannte sie alle.
4 F: Esst ihr oft Rindfleisch?
A: Nein, wir essen normalerweise entweder Huhn oder Fisch, kein Rindfleisch.

language_notes.label: I
language_notes.explanation_en:
[zh:难怪] ist ein Adverb, das als Interjektion verwendet wird, oft am Satzanfang.
1 Kein Wunder, dass er das nicht weiß, er schaut ja überhaupt keine Nachrichten!
2 Kein Wunder, dass er sich nicht für ein Graduiertenstudium bewirbt, er hat ja schon einen Job gefunden.
3 Kein Wunder, dass er in den letzten Tagen nicht gut gelaunt war, seine Freundin hat sich von ihm getrennt.

language_notes.label: J
language_notes.explanation_en:
[zh:多] kann als Intensivierung in Ausrufesätzen verwendet werden:
1 Wie schwer ist es doch für Eltern, Geld zu verdienen!
2 Xiao Ming, wie kalt es draußen ist! Sei brav und bleib drinnen, renn nicht draußen herum!
3 Wie hässlich dieser Trainingsanzug ist! Wer würde den kaufen? Ich würde ihn nicht einmal als Geschenk nehmen.

grammar_sections.title: Direktionale Ergänzungen (III)
grammar_sections.explanation_en:
Per Definition hat diese Art von Ergänzung mit Richtung zu tun: Zum Beispiel bedeutet [zh:来] eine Bewegung auf den Sprecher zu und [zh:上] eine Aufwärtsbewegung. Viele direktionale Ergänzungen befassen sich jedoch nicht im wörtlichen Sinne mit Richtung, sondern deuten ein Ergebnis oder einen Zustand an. Diese Lektion enthält direktionale Ergänzungen, die Ergebnisse ausdrücken.

[zh:下来] impliziert die Ablösung oder Trennung einer Sache von einer anderen.

[zh:出来] bedeutet das Auftauchen von etwas aus einem versteckten Ort ins Offene oder eine Veränderung von nichts zu etwas.

examples.hanzi: 他们希望孩子们能受到良好的教育, 所以孩子一生下来就开始给他们存教育费。
examples.pinyin: Tāmen xīwàng háizimen néng shòudào liánghǎo de jiàoyù, suǒyǐ háizi yì shēng xiàlái jiù kāishǐ gěi tāmen cún jiàoyù fèi.
examples.translation: Sie hofften, dass ihre Kinder eine gute Ausbildung erhalten würden. Deshalb begannen sie, sobald die Kinder geboren waren, für deren Ausbildungskosten zu sparen.

examples.hanzi: 从本子上撕下一张纸来
examples.pinyin: cóng běnzi shàng sī xià yì zhāng zhǐ lái
examples.translation: ein Blatt Papier aus einem Notizbuch reißen

examples.pinyin: bǎ xīguā qiē xiàlái yí kuài
examples.translation: eine Scheibe von einer Wassermelone schneiden

examples.hanzi: 每年开学的时候, 家里还是得拿出很多钱来。
examples.pinyin: Měi nián kāixué de shíhou, jiālǐ háishì děi ná chū hěn duō qián lái.
examples.translation: Jedes Jahr zu Beginn des Schuljahres muss meine Familie immer noch viel Geld aufbringen.

examples.hanzi: 把你的问题说出来, 我们大家帮你解决。
examples.pinyin: Bǎ nǐ de wèntí shuō chūlái, wǒmen dàjiā bāng nǐ jiějué.
examples.translation: Sprechen Sie Ihr Problem offen an. Wir helfen Ihnen, es zu lösen.

examples.pinyin: Wǒ kàn chūlái tā shēngqì le.
examples.translation: Ich merkte, dass er wütend wurde.

examples.hanzi: 给孩子想出来一个好名字。
examples.pinyin: Gěi háizi xiǎng chūlái yí ge hǎo míngzi.
examples.translation: Denk dir einen guten Namen für das Kind aus.

usage_notes: 上, 上来, 上去, 下, 下来, 下去, 出, 出来, 过, 过来, 过去, 起, 起来, 开, usw. können alle ein Ergebnis ausdrücken. Direktionale Komplemente dieser Art sind recht häufig. Versuchen Sie, sie zusammen mit den vorangehenden Verben auswendig zu lernen.

grammar_sections.title: Zwei Verbphrasen mit 来 verbinden
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 来 kann verwendet werden, um zwei Verben zu verbinden. Das zweite Verb gibt den Zweck der ersten Handlung an. 来 hat eine ähnliche Bedeutung wie „um zu“.

patterns.pattern: 动词短语1 + 来 + 动词短语2
patterns.meaning_en: VP1 tun, um VP2 zu erreichen.

examples.hanzi: 张天明觉得自己已经是大人了,应该找工作挣钱来减轻父母的负担。
examples.pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng juéde zìjǐ yījīng shì dàren le, yīnggāi zhǎo gōngzuò zhèng qián lai jiǎnqīng fùmǔ de fùdān.
examples.translation: Zhang Tianming ist der Meinung, dass er erwachsen ist und einen Job annehmen sollte, um die Last seiner Eltern zu erleichtern.

examples.hanzi: 大家计划开一个舞会来给他过生日。
examples.pinyin: Dàjiā jìhuà kāi yí ge wǔhuì lái gěi tā guò shēngrì.

grammar_sections.title: Das Partikel 了 (V)
grammar_sections.explanation_en:
Sie haben gelernt, dass 了 die Realisierung einer Handlung oder das Eintreten eines Zustands anzeigen kann. Allerdings ist 了 nicht immer bei abgeschlossenen Handlungen erforderlich. Nehmen Sie diesen Auszug aus dem Lektionstext als Beispiel:

In der obigen Passage ist 他们希望孩子们能受到良好的教育，所以孩子一生下来就开始给他们存教育费 ein abgeschlossenes Ereignis. Es wird jedoch als Teil der Hintergrundinformation zur Haupterzählung präsentiert: 这时候丽莎和雪梅走了进来. Aus diesem Grund nimmt das Verb 存 kein 了.

examples.hanzi: 张天明的父母收入虽然不少，但是供天明和姐姐上大学，经济上还是有些压力。他们希望孩子们能受到良好的教育，所以孩子一生下来就开始给他们存教育费。
examples.pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng de fùmù shōurù suīrán bù shǎo, dànshì gōng Tiānmíng hé jiějie shàng dàxué, jīngjì shang háishi yǒu xiē yālì. Tāmen xīwàng háizi men néng shòu dào liánghǎo de jiàoyù, suǒyǐ háizi yì shēng xia lai jiù kāishǐ gěi tāmen cún jiàoyù fèi.
examples.translation: Obwohl das Einkommen von Zhang Tianmings Eltern nicht unerheblich ist, übt es doch einen gewissen finanziellen Druck aus, Tianming und seine Schwester aufs College zu schicken. Sie hoffen, dass ihre Kinder eine gute Ausbildung erhalten. Deshalb begannen sie, für die Ausbildung ihrer Kinder zu sparen, als diese geboren wurden.

examples.hanzi: 这时候丽莎和雪梅走了进来。
examples.pinyin: Zhè shíhou Lìshā hé Xuěméi zǒu le jìn lai.
examples.translation: In diesem Moment kamen Lisha und Xuemei herein.

usage_notes: 了 ist nicht immer bei abgeschlossenen Handlungen erforderlich. Wenn ein abgeschlossenes Ereignis als Teil der Hintergrundinformation zur Haupterzählung präsentiert wird, nimmt das Verb oft kein 了.

grammar_sections.title: Rhetorische Fragen
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Rhetorische Fragen sind zwar in der Form interrogativ, werden aber tatsächlich verwendet, um einen Punkt zu betonen. Es sind keine echten Fragen und erfordern daher keine Antwort. Im Allgemeinen tragen rhetorische Fragen, die in der Form bejahend sind, eine nachdrücklich negative Bedeutung; rhetorische Fragen, die in der Form verneinend sind, tragen eine nachdrücklich positive Bedeutung.

examples.hanzi: 他昨天根本没复习，能考一百分吗？
examples.pinyin: Tā zuótiān gēnběn méi fùxí, néng kǎo yìbǎi fēn ma?
examples.translation: Er hat gestern überhaupt nicht gelernt. Wie sollte er da hundert Punkte im Test bekommen?

examples.hanzi: 他已经学了三年化学了，这么简单的问题他不会吗？
examples.pinyin: Tā yǐjīng xuéle sān nián huàxuéle, zhème jiǎndān de wèntí tā bú huì ma?
examples.translation: Er studiert seit drei Jahren Chemie. Soll er dieses einfache Problem nicht lösen können?

examples.hanzi: 她怎么不想想，父母挣钱多不容易啊？
examples.pinyin: Tā zěnme bù xiǎngxiang, fùmǔ zhèngqián duō bù róngyì a?
examples.translation: Wie kommt es, dass sie sich keine Gedanken darüber macht, wie schwer es für ihre Eltern ist, Geld zu verdienen?

examples.hanzi: 谁不知道你是父母的乖孩子！

examples.translation: Wer weiß nicht, dass du ein vorbildliches Kind bist?

global_config.setting_summary_cn: 故事发生在一间典型的大学男生宿舍，以及通过蒙太奇展现的家庭生活片段。整体氛围温馨而现实。
global_config.setting_summary_en: The story takes place in a typical male college dorm room, with montage scenes of family life. The overall atmosphere is warm and realistic.

character_definitions.name: 张天明
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A college-age Asian male, around 20 years old, with short black hair, wearing a casual hoodie and jeans. Looks thoughtful and responsible.

character_definitions.name: 雪梅
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A college-age Asian female, around 20 years old, with long dark hair and glasses, wearing a simple, neat blouse. Looks studious and gentle.

character_definitions.name: 父母
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A middle-aged Asian couple, around 50 years old, dressed in simple, everyday clothes. They look kind, hardworking, and slightly worried when looking at bills.

hanzi: 张天明的父母希望孩子们能受到良好的教育,所以孩子一生下来就开始给他们存教育费。
虽然张天明父母的收入不少,但是供天明和姐姐上大学,经济上还是有些压力。
张天明觉得自己已经是大人了,应该找工作挣钱来减轻父母的负担,所以就上网看看有没有适合自己的工作。
pinyin: zhāng tiān míng de fù mǔ xī wàng hái zi men néng shòu dào liáng hǎo de jiào yù suǒ yǐ hái zi yī shēng xià lái jiù kāi shǐ gěi tā men cún jiào yù fèi
suī rán zhāng tiān míng fù mǔ de shōu rù bù shǎo dàn shì gōng tiān míng hé jiě jie shàng dà xué jīng jì shàng hái shì yǒu xiē yā lì
zhāng tiān míng jué de zì jǐ yǐ jīng shì dà ren le yīng gāi zhǎo gōng zuò zhèng qián lái jiǎn qīng fù mǔ de fù dān suǒ yǐ jiù shàng wǎng kàn kan yǒu méi yǒu shì hé zì jǐ de gōng zuò
translation: Zhang Tianming's parents hope their children can receive a good education, so they started saving for their education fund as soon as they were born.
Although Zhang Tianming's parents' income is not low, supporting him and his older sister through college still puts some financial pressure on them.
Zhang Tianming feels that he is already an adult and should find a job to earn money to lessen his parents' burden, so he went online to see if there were any suitable jobs.

scenes.scene_goal: 建立张天明想打工的动机，展现他体谅父母的孝心。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 【蒙太奇】一组快切镜头：1. 一双年轻父母的手把钱放进一个写着“教育费”的存钱罐。2. 多年后，这对父母（已中年）看着账单，面露愁容。3. 切换到大学宿舍，张天明看着桌上父母的照片，眼神变得坚定，然后打开笔记本电脑，开始浏览招聘网站。
scenes.setting_en: Montage: A family home, then a college dorm room.
scenes.shot_type: Close-up on hands and faces, Medium shot of Tianming.
scenes.video_prompt_en: A montage in slice-of-life anime style. First, a close-up of young parents' hands putting money into a piggy bank labeled 'Education Fund'. Then, a shot of the now middle-aged parents looking at bills with worried expressions. Finally, a medium shot of their son, Zhang Tianming, in his dorm room. He looks at a photo of his parents, his expression becomes determined, and he opens his laptop to a job search website.

voice_over.speaker: 旁白
voice_over.hanzi: 张天明觉得自己已经是大人了,应该找工作挣钱来减轻父母的负担,所以就上网看看有没有适合自己的工作。
voice_over.pinyin: zhāng tiān míng jué de zì jǐ yǐ jīng shì dà ren le yīng gāi zhǎo gōng zuò zhèng qián lái jiǎn qīng fù mǔ de fù dān suǒ yǐ jiù shàng wǎng kàn kan yǒu méi yǒu shì hé zì jǐ de gōng zuò
voice_over.translation: Zhang Tianming feels that he is already an adult and should find a job to earn money to lessen his parents' burden, so he went online to see if there were any suitable jobs.

dǎgōng huì bú huì yǐngxiǎng xuéxí
translation: Just then, Lisha and Xuemei walked in.
Tianming, what are you looking at online?
I want to get a part-time job to spend less of my family's money.
Will working part-time affect your studies?

scenes.scene_goal: 引入新角色，并通过对话引出“打工是否会影响学习”的核心议题。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 宿舍门被推开，丽莎和雪梅走了进来。丽莎好奇地凑到天明身后，看他的电脑屏幕。天明转过身回答她。听到“打工”，雪梅立刻露出担忧的表情并提问。
scenes.camera_movement: Camera follows Lisha and Xuemei as they enter, then focuses on the three-person interaction.
scenes.video_prompt_en: In the dorm room, the door opens and Lisha and Xuemei walk in. Lisha cheerfully walks behind Tianming and peeks at his laptop screen, asking what he's doing. Tianming turns his chair to face them and explains he wants a part-time job. Xuemei, standing by the door, immediately shows a concerned expression and asks if it will affect his studies.

voice_over.speaker: 天明
voice_over.hanzi: 我想打点儿工,少花一点儿家里的钱。
voice_over.pinyin: wǒ xiǎng dǎ diǎnr gōng shǎo huā yìdiǎnr jiālǐ de qián
voice_over.translation: I want to get a part-time job to spend less of my family's money.

hanzi: 我想打工是想取得一些工作经验。如果打工时间不太多，不会影响学习。
我也想打工挣点儿零用钱。
pinyin: wǒ xiǎng dǎgōng shì xiǎng qǔdé yìxiē gōngzuò jīngyàn rúguǒ dǎgōng shíjiān bú tài duō bú huì yǐngxiǎng xuéxí
wǒ yě xiǎng dǎgōng zhèng diǎnr língyòngqián
nǐ bú shì yǒu jiǎngxuéjīn ma
translation: I want to work to gain some work experience. If the hours aren't too long, it won't affect my studies.
I also want to get a part-time job to earn some pocket money.

scenes.scene_goal: 展现不同角色对打工的不同看法：获取经验 vs 挣零用钱。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 天明自信地向雪梅解释自己的想法。一旁的丽莎听到后，眼睛一亮，兴奋地表示自己也想打工。天明有些惊讶地看着丽莎，问起她的奖学金。
scenes.shot_type: Medium close-up on each character as they speak.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Tianming confidently explains to Xuemei that he wants work experience and will manage his time. Hearing this, Lisha's eyes light up, and she excitedly says she also wants to work for pocket money. Tianming looks at Lisha with a surprised expression and asks about her scholarship.

voice_over.speaker: 丽莎
voice_over.hanzi: 我也想打工挣点儿零用钱。
voice_over.pinyin: wǒ yě xiǎng dǎgōng zhèng diǎnr língyòngqián
voice_over.translation: I also want to get a part-time job to earn some pocket money.

hanzi: 奖学金不够交学费,我还申请了政府的学生贷款。雪梅,中国大学生也打工吗?
以前不用,因为那时候上大学不用交学费。现在大学收费了,所以不少大学生也想办法打工挣钱。
pinyin: jiǎngxuéjīn bú gòu jiāo xuéfèi wǒ hái shēnqǐng le zhèngfǔ de xuésheng dàikuǎn Xuěméi Zhōngguó dàxuéshēng yě dǎgōng ma
yǐqián bú yòng yīnwèi nà shíhou shàng dàxué bú yòng jiāo xuéfèi xiànzài dàxué shōufèi le suǒyǐ bùshǎo dàxuéshēng yě xiǎng bànfǎ dǎgōng zhèngqián
translation: The scholarship isn't enough to cover tuition, and I've also applied for a government student loan. Xuemei, do Chinese college students also work part-time?
They didn't used to, because back then, college was free. Now colleges charge tuition, so many students also try to find ways to work and earn money.

scenes.scene_goal: 解释学生打工的经济原因，并提供关于中国大学情况的文化背景信息。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎解释了她的经济状况，然后好奇地转向雪梅，询问中国大学的情况。雪梅推了推眼镜，开始向两位外国朋友介绍中国大学生打工情况的变化。
scenes.camera_movement: Camera focuses on Lisha, then shifts to Xuemei as she becomes the main speaker.
scenes.shot_type: Medium shot, focusing on the group listening to Xuemei.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Lisha explains that her scholarship isn't enough for tuition and she also has a student loan. She then turns to Xuemei and asks if Chinese students also work. Xuemei thoughtfully pushes up her glasses and explains how things have changed since Chinese universities started charging tuition.

voice_over.speaker: 雪梅
voice_over.hanzi: 以前不用,因为那时候上大学不用交学费。现在大学收费了,所以不少大学生也想办法打工挣钱。
voice_over.pinyin: yǐqián bú yòng yīnwèi nà shíhou shàng dàxué bú yòng jiāo xuéfèi xiànzài dàxué shōufèi le suǒyǐ bùshǎo dàxuéshēng yě xiǎng bànfǎ dǎgōng zhèngqián
voice_over.translation: They didn't used to, because back then, college was free. Now colleges charge tuition, so many students also try to find ways to work and earn money.

hanzi: 说到钱,我的同屋今天跟我借钱。她说她妈妈生气了,不给她钱了。
pinyin: shuōdào qián wǒ de tóngwū jīntiān gēn wǒ jièqián tā shuō tā māma shēngqì le bù gěi tā qián le
translation: Speaking of money, my roommate borrowed money from me today. She said her mom got angry and wouldn't give her any more money.

scenes.scene_goal: 引出关于丽莎室友财务问题的话题，天明的好奇心被勾起。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎表情有点无奈地向朋友们提起室友借钱的事，并解释了原因。天明立刻好奇地追问。
scenes.setting_en: A bright, modern campus cafe with students in the background.
scenes.camera_movement: Static shot, then a quick rack focus to Tianming.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Cinematic medium shot of three college students at a cafe table. A girl (Lisa) leans forward, speaking with a troubled expression. The camera then quickly shifts focus to a boy (Tianming) opposite her as he leans in and asks a question with a curious look. Natural lighting from a large window.

voice_over.speaker: 丽莎
voice_over.hanzi: 说到钱,我的同屋今天跟我借钱。她说她妈妈生气了,不给她钱了。
voice_over.pinyin: shuōdào qián wǒ de tóngwū jīntiān gēn wǒ jièqián tā shuō tā māma shēngqì le bù gěi tā qián le
voice_over.translation: Speaking of money, my roommate borrowed money from me today. She said her mom got angry and wouldn't give her any more money.

hanzi: 她父母把她这个学期的饭钱都交了,可是她不是叫外卖,就是跟同学去饭馆儿,还常常乱买东西,乱花钱,欠了银行和信用卡公司很多钱。
上个月父母给的零用钱,她不到十天就花完了,又跟妈妈要了一些,还是不够。
pinyin: tā fù mǔ bǎ tā zhè ge xué qī de fàn qián dōu jiāo le kě shì tā bú shì jiào wài mài jiù shì gēn tóng xué qù fàn guǎnr hái cháng cháng luàn mǎi dōng xi luàn huā qián qiàn le yín háng hé xìn yòng kǎ gōng sī hěn duō qián
shàng ge yuè fù mǔ gěi de líng yòng qián tā bú dào shí tiān jiù huā wán le yòu gēn mā ma yào le yì xiē hái shì bú gòu
translation: Her parents already paid for her meals for the semester, but she either orders takeout or goes to restaurants with classmates. She also often buys things impulsively, spends money recklessly, and owes a lot of money to the bank and credit card companies.
The pocket money her parents gave her last month, she spent it all in less than ten days, and then asked her mom for more, but it still wasn't enough.

scenes.scene_goal: 通过快速剪辑的闪回画面，生动展示丽莎室友的消费习惯。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 随着丽莎的讲述，画面闪回，呈现她室友花钱如流水的场景：手机上点外卖，和朋友在餐厅大吃大喝，疯狂购物，最后看着信用卡账单发愁。日历飞速翻过，钱包变空。
scenes.setting_en: Montage of scenes: a messy dorm room with takeout containers, a lively restaurant, a trendy clothing store, a girl looking stressed at credit card statements on her laptop.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Dynamic montage sequence with a voice-over. 1. Close-up on a phone screen showing a food delivery app. 2. A girl (roommate) laughing with friends in a fancy restaurant. 3. The same girl carrying multiple shopping bags. 4. A calendar's pages flipping by rapidly. 5. The girl looking stressed while staring at credit card bills on a laptop screen, surrounded by her purchases.

voice_over.speaker: 丽莎
voice_over.hanzi: 她父母把她这个学期的饭钱都交了,可是她不是叫外卖,就是跟同学去饭馆儿,还常常乱买东西,乱花钱,欠了银行和信用卡公司很多钱。上个月父母给的零用钱,她不到十天就花完了,又跟妈妈要了一些,还是不够。
voice_over.pinyin: tā fù mǔ bǎ tā zhè ge xué qī de fàn qián dōu jiāo le kě shì tā bú shì jiào wài mài jiù shì gēn tóng xué qù fàn guǎnr hái cháng cháng luàn mǎi dōng xi luàn huā qián qiàn le yín háng hé xìn yòng kǎ gōng sī hěn duō qián。shàng ge yuè fù mǔ gěi de líng yòng qián tā bú dào shí tiān jiù huā wán le yòu gēn mā ma yào le yì xiē hái shì bú gòu
voice_over.translation: Her parents already paid for her meals for the semester, but she either orders takeout or goes to restaurants with classmates. She also often buys things impulsively, spends money recklessly, and owes a lot of money to the bank and credit card companies. The pocket money her parents gave her last month, she spent it all in less than ten days, and then asked her mom for more, but it still wasn't enough.

hanzi: 难怪她妈妈生气。她怎么不想想,父母挣钱多不容易啊!
是啊,我们不应该乱花钱。
pinyin: nánguài tā māma shēngqì tā zěnme bù xiǎngxiang fùmǔ zhèngqián duō bù róngyì a
shì a wǒmen bù yīnggāi luàn huāqián
translation: No wonder her mom is angry. Why doesn't she consider how hard it is for her parents to earn money!
Yeah, we shouldn't spend money recklessly.

scenes.scene_goal: 天明和雪梅对室友的行为表达看法，引出“挣钱不容易”和“不应乱花钱”的观点。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 镜头切回咖啡馆。天明听完后，摇着头感叹，对室友的行为表示不解。雪梅也严肃地附和，表示赞同。
scenes.camera_movement: Slow pan from Tianming to Xuemei.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Cinematic medium close-up, back at the cafe. A boy (Tianming) shakes his head with a look of disapproval and empathy. The camera slowly pans to a girl with glasses (Xuemei) next to him, who nods seriously in agreement. Lisa is visible in the frame, listening to their reactions.

voice_over.speaker: 天明
voice_over.hanzi: 难怪她妈妈生气。她怎么不想想,父母挣钱多不容易啊!
voice_over.pinyin: nánguài tā māma shēngqì tā zěnme bù xiǎngxiang fùmǔ zhèngqián duō bù róngyì a
voice_over.translation: No wonder her mom is angry. Why doesn't she consider how hard it is for her parents to earn money!

hanzi: 谁不知道你是父母的乖孩子!好吧,你慢慢儿找工作,我们走了。
pinyin: shéi bù zhīdào nǐ shì fùmǔ de guāi háizi hǎo ba nǐ mànmànr zhǎo gōngzuò wǒmen zǒu le
translation: Everyone knows you're your parents' good kid! Alright, you take your time looking for a job. We're leaving.

scenes.scene_goal: 丽莎以轻松的方式结束话题，并和天明准备离开。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 丽莎听到雪梅一本正经的评论，俏皮地开她玩笑，说她是“乖孩子”。然后她站起来，示意天明一起走，留下雪梅一个人继续在电脑上找工作。
scenes.camera_movement: Static, then follows Lisa and Tianming as they walk away.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Medium shot in the cafe. A girl (Lisa) playfully smiles and teases her friend (Xuemei). Then, Lisa stands up and gestures to the boy (Tianming) to leave. They both get up, wave goodbye to Xuemei, and walk out of frame. The camera holds on Xuemei, who smiles and turns back to her laptop.

voice_over.speaker: 丽莎
voice_over.hanzi: 谁不知道你是父母的乖孩子!好吧,你慢慢儿找工作,我们走了。
voice_over.pinyin: shéi bù zhīdào nǐ shì fùmǔ de guāi háizi hǎo ba nǐ mànmànr zhǎo gōngzuò wǒmen zǒu le
voice_over.translation: Everyone knows you're your parents' good kid! Alright, you take your time looking for a job. We're leaving.

global_config.teaching_style: A clear, encouraging, and structured explanation, breaking down sentences and vocabulary for foundational understanding. The tone is that of a friendly and knowledgeable teacher.
global_config.visual_style: Clean, modern educational cards with animated text and icons.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the first part of the sentence about parents' wishes for their children's education and learn the words 'shòudào' and 'liánghǎo'.

narration.subtitle_en: The lesson begins by introducing Zhang Tianming's family background. This first clause says his parents hope their children can receive a good education. The verb [zh:受到] means 'to receive,' and it's often used with abstract nouns like education. [zh:良好] is a more formal way to say 'good' or 'fine.' So, [zh:受到良好的教育] means 'to receive a good education'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 张天明的父母希望孩子们能受到良好的教育,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng de fùmǔ xīwàng háizimen néng shòudào liánghǎo de jiàoyù,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Zhang Tianming's parents hope their children can receive a good education,

highlight_words.explanation_en: Often followed by an abstract noun, like welcome (欢迎), education (教育), or influence (影响). Different from 收到 (shōu dào), which is for concrete objects.
A literary or more formal adjective for 'good', often used to describe conditions, education, or relationships.
Can be used as a noun for 'education' or a verb 'to educate'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate an icon of parents looking hopefully at a graduation cap.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the second part of the sentence about saving money and learn the verb 'cún'.

narration.subtitle_en: This part of the sentence explains the result of their hopes. It says, 'so they started saving for their education fund as soon as they were born.' The key phrase here is [zh:一生下来], which means 'as soon as one is born.' The verb [zh:存] means 'to save' or 'to deposit,' as in depositing money in a bank. So, [zh:存教育费] means to save up for education fees.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 所以孩子一生下来就开始给他们存教育费。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: suǒyǐ háizi yī shēng xiàlái jiù kāishǐ gěi tāmen cún jiàoyù fèi.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: so they started saving for their education fund as soon as they were born.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Means to leave something for safekeeping, like depositing money (存钱) or checking a bag (存包).

segments.visual_notes: Show a piggy bank with a graduation cap icon on it, with coins dropping in.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the 'suīrán' structure and the word 'shōurù'.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence starts with [zh:虽然], which means 'although,' setting up a contrast. It says, 'Although Zhang Tianming's parents' income is not low...' The word for income is [zh:收入]. Notice the structure: [zh:虽然]... [zh:但是]... which means 'although... but...'. This first part establishes that the family is not poor.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 虽然张天明父母的收入不少,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Suīrán Zhāng Tiānmíng fùmǔ de shōurù bù shǎo,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Although Zhang Tianming's parents' income is not low,

highlight_words.explanation_en: The money received, especially on a regular basis, for work or through investments.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of 收 (shōu), meaning 'to receive,' and 入 (rù), meaning 'to enter.' So, it's the money that 'comes in'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a wallet that looks reasonably full.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the concept of financial pressure and learn the words 'gōng' and 'yālì'.

narration.subtitle_en: Here's the contrast, starting with [zh:但是], meaning 'but.' It explains that supporting Tianming and his sister through college still creates some financial pressure. The verb [zh:供] here means 'to provide financial support for.' The key noun is [zh:压力], which means 'pressure.' So, [zh:有压力] means 'to have pressure' or 'to be under pressure'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 但是供天明和姐姐上大学,经济上还是有些压力。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: dànshì gōng Tiānmíng hé jiějie shàng dàxué, jīngjì shàng háishì yǒuxiē yālì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: but supporting him and his older sister through college still puts some financial pressure on them.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can mean to supply (like water) or to provide financial support, as in supporting a child's education.
A noun for pressure or stress. Common phrases are 有压力 (to have pressure) and 给...压力 (to give...pressure).

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a person being squeezed by two arrows labeled '$$'.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand Tianming's motivation to work and learn 'dàren', 'jiǎnqīng', and 'fùdān'.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence explains Tianming's perspective. He feels he's already an [zh:大人], an 'adult,' and should find a job to lessen his parents' burden. The verb [zh:减轻] means 'to lessen' or 'alleviate.' It's often used with abstract nouns like 'burden' or 'pressure.' The word for burden is [zh:负担]. The particle [zh:来] connects the two actions, showing that 'earning money' is the means 'in order to' lessen the burden.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 张天明觉得自己已经是大人了,应该找工作挣钱来减轻父母的负担,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng juéde zìjǐ yǐjīng shì dàren le, yīnggāi zhǎo gōngzuò zhèngqián lái jiǎnqīng fùmǔ de fùdān,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Zhang Tianming feels that he is already an adult and should find a job to earn money to lessen his parents' burden,

highlight_words.explanation_en: The opposite is 小孩 (xiǎohái), 'child.' Be careful not to say 小人 (xiǎorén), which means a despicable person.
To reduce or alleviate. Composed of 减 (jiǎn) 'to reduce' and 轻 (qīng) 'light'.
A physical or metaphorical load. Composed of 负 (fù) 'to carry on the back' and 担 (dān) 'to carry on the shoulder'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a character lifting a heavy weight off of their parents' shoulders.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the action Tianming takes and learn the verb 'shìhé'.

narration.subtitle_en: This clause shows what Tianming does next. 'So he went online to see if there were any suitable jobs.' The phrase [zh:上网] means 'to go online.' The key verb here is [zh:适合], which means 'to suit.' He is looking for a job that suits him. We'll look at this word more closely in a moment.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 所以就上网看看有没有适合自己的工作。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: suǒyǐ jiù shàngwǎng kànkan yǒu méiyǒu shìhé zìjǐ de gōngzuò.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: so he went online to see if there were any suitable jobs.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb that must be followed by a noun. It describes something that is a good fit for someone or something.

segments.visual_notes: Show a character on a laptop with a magnifying glass over job listings.

segments.segment_title: Usage: 适合 vs. 合适
segments.teaching_goal: Learn the difference between the verb 'shìhé' (to suit) and the adjective 'héshì' (suitable).

narration.subtitle_en: Let's clarify the difference between [zh:适合] and [zh:合适]. [zh:适合] is a verb, meaning 'to suit,' so it must be followed by an object. For example, [zh:这个工作适合我] means 'This job suits me.' On the other hand, [zh:合适] is an adjective, meaning 'suitable.' You would use it to describe something, like [zh:这个工作很合适], 'This job is very suitable.' So, remember: verb versus adjective.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Correct: 这个工作适合你。 / 这个工作很合适。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhège gōngzuò shìhé nǐ. / Zhège gōngzuò hěn héshì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Correct: This job suits you. / This job is very suitable.
on_screen_text.notes: 适合 (shìhé) is a verb. 合适 (héshì) is an adjective.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A transitive verb that must be followed by a noun object. Answers 'What does it suit?'
An adjective that describes a noun as being suitable or appropriate. Answers 'Is it suitable?'

segments.visual_notes: Use a checkmark for the correct example and an 'X' for a potential incorrect usage like '这个工作很适合'.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand a simple narrative sentence that moves the story forward.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a simple sentence to transition the scene. It means, 'Just then, Lisha and Xuemei walked in.' [zh:这时候] means 'at this moment.' The phrase [zh:走了进来] is a directional complement, where [zh:走] is 'to walk' and [zh:进来] indicates movement 'in' and 'toward' the speaker. It vividly describes their entrance.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这时候丽莎和雪梅走了进来。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhè shíhou Lìshā hé Xuěméi zǒu le jìnlái.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Just then, Lisha and Xuemei walked in.

segments.visual_notes: Animate two characters walking through a doorway.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand a common question about online activity.

narration.subtitle_en: Lisha asks Tianming a question: 'Tianming, what are you looking at online?' Let's break it down. [zh:网上] means 'on the internet' or 'online.' [zh:看什么] means 'what are you looking at?' The particle [zh:呢] at the end of the question softens the tone and implies the action is ongoing.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 天明,你在网上看什么呢?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tiānmíng, nǐ zài wǎngshàng kàn shénme ne?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Tianming, what are you looking at online?

segments.visual_notes: Show a speech bubble coming from Lisha towards Tianming, who is at his computer.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the phrase for getting a part-time job, 'dǎgōng'.

narration.subtitle_en: Tianming explains his goal. 'I want to get a part-time job to spend less of my family's money.' The key phrase here is [zh:打点儿工]. [zh:打工] is the common term for 'to work a part-time job' or 'to do manual labor for pay.' The [zh:点儿] just makes it sound more casual, like 'do a bit of work.' His reason is to [zh:少花一点儿家里的钱], 'spend a little less of the family's money.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我想打点儿工,少花一点儿家里的钱。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng dǎ diǎnr gōng, shǎo huā yìdiǎnr jiālǐ de qián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I want to get a part-time job to spend less of my family's money.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to working at a temporary or hourly-wage job, common for students.

segments.visual_notes: Animate an icon of a student working at a cafe, with money flowing away from a house icon.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to form a yes-no question with 'huì bú huì' and understand the word 'yǐngxiǎng'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a common concern for students: 'Will working part-time affect your studies?' The question uses the [zh:会不 会] structure, which is a way to ask 'will or will not...?' The verb [zh:影响] means 'to affect' or 'to influence.' It can be both a verb, as it is here, or a noun meaning 'influence.' So the question is literally, 'Working part-time will or will not affect studies?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 打工会不会影响学习？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Dǎgōng huì bú huì yǐngxiǎng xuéxí?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Will working part-time affect your studies?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can be used as a verb ('to affect') or a noun ('influence').

segments.visual_notes: Show a split screen: one side with a 'work' icon, the other with a 'books' icon, and a question mark in between.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the motivation of gaining work experience and learn 'qǔdé' and 'jīngyàn'.

narration.subtitle_en: Tianming clarifies his main reason and sets a condition. He says, 'I want to work to gain some work experience. If the hours aren't too long, it won't affect my studies.' The verb [zh:取得] means 'to obtain' or 'to gain,' and it's used with abstract nouns. Here, he wants to gain [zh:工作经验], 'work experience.' He then uses [zh:如果] ('if') to reassure his friends that he will manage his time.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我想打工是想取得一些工作经验。如果打工时间不太多，不会影响学习。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng dǎgōng shì xiǎng qǔdé yìxiē gōngzuò jīngyàn. Rúguǒ dǎgōng shíjiān bú tài duō, bú huì yǐngxiǎng xuéxí.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I want to work to gain some work experience. If the hours aren't too long, it won't affect my studies.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used with abstract nouns like experience (经验), success (成功), or progress (进步).
Knowledge or skill from doing, seeing, or feeling things.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a character collecting stars labeled 'Experience'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the terms for earning pocket money, 'zhèng' and 'língyòngqián'.

narration.subtitle_en: Now Xuemei chimes in with her own reason for wanting to work. She says, 'I also want to get a part-time job to earn some pocket money.' The verb [zh:挣] means 'to earn.' And [zh:零用钱] is the specific term for 'pocket money' or 'spending money.' It's literally 'miscellaneous-use money.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我也想打工挣点儿零用钱。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ yě xiǎng dǎgōng zhèng diǎnr língyòngqián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I also want to get a part-time job to earn some pocket money.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Specifically refers to earning money through labor or work.
Allowance or spending money. Composed of 零 (líng, 'miscellaneous'), 用 (yòng, 'use'), and 钱 (qián, 'money').

segments.visual_notes: Show a character putting coins into their pocket.

segments.teaching_goal: Recognize the 'búshì...ma?' rhetorical question structure and learn the word 'jiǎngxuéjīn'.

narration.subtitle_en: Tianming asks a rhetorical question, which is a question that doesn't need an answer because the speaker thinks they already know it. He asks, 'Don't you have a scholarship?' The structure [zh:不是...吗] implies surprise and is used to state something emphatically. He's really saying, 'But I thought you have a scholarship!' The word for scholarship is [zh:奖学金].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你不是有奖学金吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ búshì yǒu jiǎngxuéjīn ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Don't you have a scholarship?
on_screen_text.notes: This is a rhetorical question implying: 'I thought you had a scholarship!'

highlight_words.explanation_en: Money awarded to a student to help pay for education. 奖 (jiǎng) means 'award', 学 (xué) means 'study', and 金 (jīn) means 'money'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a certificate icon with a '$' sign on it, with a large question mark next to it.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to talk about tuition, student loans, and ask about part-time work culture.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker starts by explaining their financial situation. They say [zh:奖学金不够交学费], meaning 'The scholarship isn't enough to cover tuition.' Then, they add, [zh:我还申请了政府的学生贷款], 'I've also applied for a government student loan.' The key verbs are [zh:申请] for 'to apply' and [zh:贷款] for 'loan'. Finally, they ask Xuemei a question to compare cultures: [zh:中国大学生也打工吗], 'Do Chinese college students also work part-time?' The word [zh:打工] is the key term for working a part-time or manual labor job.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 奖学金不够交学费,我还申请了政府的学生贷款。雪梅,中国大学生也打工吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Jiǎngxuéjīn bú gòu jiāo xuéfèi, wǒ hái shēnqǐng le zhèngfǔ de xuéshēng dàikuǎn. Xuěméi, Zhōngguó dàxuéshēng yě dǎgōng ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The scholarship isn't enough to cover tuition, and I've also applied for a government student loan. Xuemei, do Chinese college students also work part-time?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'encourage-learning-money', it refers to academic scholarships.
A verb used for formal applications, like for a job, loan, or school.
A noun or verb related to borrowing money, often from a bank or institution.
Refers to working a temporary or hourly job, often for students or as a side job.

segments.visual_notes: Animate icons for 'scholarship', 'loan application', and 'part-time job' to illustrate the concepts.

segments.segment_title: Part-Time Work in China: Then and Now
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to contrast the past and present regarding the necessity of part-time work for students in China.

narration.subtitle_en: Xuemei explains the change over time. She starts with [zh:以前不用], 'They didn't used to.' Why? [zh:因为那时候上大学不用交学费], 'because back then, college was free.' Now, the situation is different: [zh:现在大学收费了], 'Now colleges charge tuition.' The verb [zh:收费] means 'to charge a fee'. Because of this change, [zh:所以不少大学生也想办法打工挣钱], 'so many students also try to find ways to work and earn money.' The phrase [zh:想办法] means 'to think of a way,' and [zh:挣钱] means 'to earn money.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 以前不用,因为那时候上大学不用交学费。现在大学收费了,所以不少大学生也想办法打工挣钱。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Yǐqián bú yòng, yīnwèi nà shíhou shàng dàxué bú yòng jiāo xuéfèi. Xiànzài dàxué shōufèi le, suǒyǐ bù shǎo dàxuéshēng yě xiǎng bànfǎ dǎgōng zhèngqián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: They didn't used to, because back then, college was free. Now colleges charge tuition, so many students also try to find ways to work and earn money.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A time word used to talk about past events or situations.
A verb indicating that a service or product costs money.
A common phrase meaning to figure out a solution to a problem.
A verb specifically for earning money through labor or work.

segments.visual_notes: Use a split screen or timeline visual to contrast '以前' (past) with '现在' (present).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask about common types of jobs and locations for part-time work.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, the speaker asks for more specific details. The first question is [zh:中国大学生一般在哪儿打工], 'Where do Chinese college students usually work?' The word [zh:一般] means 'generally' or 'usually'. Then, they offer a guess: [zh:在餐馆儿当服务员吗], 'As waiters in restaurants?' Notice the use of [zh:当], which means 'to work as' or 'to be' in the context of a role or profession. [zh:服务员] is the word for a waiter or service staff.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 中国大学生一般在哪儿打工?在餐馆儿当服务员吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhōngguó dàxuéshēng yìbān zài nǎr dǎgōng? Zài cānguǎnr dāng fúwùyuán ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Where do Chinese college students usually work? As waiters in restaurants?

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb used to describe what is common or typical.
A common word for restaurant, with the 'er' sound typical of northern Chinese dialects.
A verb used to indicate one's role or job title.
A compound word: '服务' (service) + '员' (personnel).

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a student with a question mark, pointing to icons of different jobs like a restaurant, office, etc.

segments.segment_title: Why Students Don't Often Work in Restaurants
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the socio-economic reasons provided for why certain jobs are less common for students.

narration.subtitle_en: Xuemei clarifies that while some students work in restaurants, it's not many, [zh:但是很少]. She then explains the reason. In Chinese cities, there are many people from the countryside, [zh:从农村出来], looking for work. Restaurants, or [zh:饭馆儿], prefer to hire them, [zh:喜欢找他们]. The reason given is economic: [zh:因为工资比较低], 'because their wages are lower.' This provides a glimpse into the labor market dynamics in China.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 在餐馆儿打工的大学生也有,但是很少。中国城市里有很多从农村出来找工作的人,饭馆儿喜欢找他们,因为工资比较低。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zài cānguǎnr dǎgōng de dàxuéshēng yě yǒu, dànshì hěn shǎo. Zhōngguó chéngshì lǐ yǒu hěn duō cóng nóngcūn chūlái zhǎo gōngzuò de rén, fànguǎnr xǐhuān zhǎo tāmen, yīnwèi gōngzī bǐjiào dī.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: There are some college students who work in restaurants, but not many. In Chinese cities, there are many people from the countryside looking for work, and restaurants like to hire them because their wages are lower.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates a small quantity or low frequency.
Literally 'agriculture village', it refers to areas outside of cities.
The money earned from a job. '工' means work and '资' relates to funds.

segments.visual_notes: Show a simple diagram: a restaurant icon choosing between a worker from the '农村' icon and a '大学生' (student) icon, with a 'lower wage' symbol.

segments.segment_title: Usage: 工资 vs. 薪水
segments.teaching_goal: To distinguish between the two common words for 'salary' and know which is more common in Mainland China.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at the word for 'wages', [zh:工资]. There's another word, [zh:薪水], which also means 'salary'. While both are correct, their usage frequency differs. In Mainland China, [zh:工资] is used much more often in everyday conversation and formal contexts. [zh:薪水] might be heard more in Taiwan or in more literary or formal business settings. For most learners, mastering [zh:工资] is the priority.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 工资 / 薪水
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: gōngzī / xīnshui
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: wages, salary
on_screen_text.notes: In Mainland China, 工资 is used much more often than 薪水.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The more common term for salary in Mainland China.
A less common, slightly more formal term for salary.

segments.visual_notes: Display '工资' prominently, with '薪水' shown as a secondary alternative. Use a map of China to highlight where '工资' is most common.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask about past experiences using the particle '过'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a very direct and useful question: [zh:你打工过吗]. It means, 'Have you ever worked a part-time job?' The key element here is the particle [zh:过], placed after the verb [zh:打工]. This particle is used to talk about past actions or experiences, indicating that something has happened at least once before. It's the standard way to form 'Have you ever...?' questions.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你打工过吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ dǎgōng guo ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Have you ever worked a part-time job?
on_screen_text.notes: The particle '过' (guo) indicates a past experience.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Placed after a verb to indicate that the action has been experienced in the past.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the character '过' and show it being added after the verb '打工'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn vocabulary for various common part-time jobs for students.

narration.subtitle_en: Xuemei answers yes, [zh:打过], 'I have.' She then lists several examples. Her own job was a tutor, [zh:我做过家教]. She also mentions her classmates' jobs: some do translation, [zh:有的做翻译]; some manage computers, [zh:有的管理电脑]; and others help people walk dogs, [zh:遛狗], or feed cats, [zh:喂猫]. This gives you a great list of vocabulary for different types of student work.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 打过,我做过家教,教英文。我的同学有的做翻译,有的管理电脑,也有的帮人遛狗或者喂猫。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Dǎ guo, wǒ zuò guo jiājiào, jiāo Yīngwén. Wǒ de tóngxué yǒu de zuò fānyì, yǒu de guǎnlǐ diànnǎo, yě yǒu de bāng rén liù gǒu huòzhě wèi māo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Yes, I have. I've been a tutor, teaching English. Some of my classmates do translation, some manage computers, and some help people walk dogs or feed cats.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Short for '家庭教师' (family teacher), a very common student job.
Can be a noun for the job/person or a verb for the action.
Used for managing systems, people, or projects.
A verb-object phrase: '遛' (to stroll) + '狗' (dog).

segments.visual_notes: Show icons for each job mentioned: a book (tutor), speech bubbles (translation), a computer, and a dog on a leash.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '一边...一边...' structure for describing simultaneous actions.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence is a summary of the discussion. It starts with [zh:看起来], which means 'it seems' or 'it looks like'. The core of the sentence is the [zh:一边...一边...] structure, used for doing two things at once. Here, the two actions are [zh:读书], 'studying', and [zh:打工挣钱], 'working to earn money'. So, the full meaning is, 'It seems a lot of students are studying and working part-time at the same time.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 看起来不少学生都是一边读书一边打工挣钱。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Kàn qǐlái bù shǎo xuéshēng dōu shì yìbiān dúshū yìbiān dǎgōng zhèngqián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: It seems like a lot of students are studying and working part-time at the same time to earn money.
on_screen_text.notes: 一边 A 一边 B: doing A and B at the same time.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A phrase used to make an observation or state an impression.
A structure used to connect two simultaneous actions.

highlight_words.translation: to study, to read a book
highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'read book', it's a common term for 'to study' or 'to attend school'.

segments.visual_notes: Visually connect two actions (e.g., an icon of a book and an icon of a money bag) with the '一边...一边...' structure.

segments.segment_title: Changing the Topic: Speaking of Money
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use '说到' to transition topics and to talk about borrowing money.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, the topic shifts. The speaker uses the phrase [zh:说到钱], 'Speaking of money...'. This is a very natural way to transition to a related topic. She then talks about her roommate, [zh:同屋], who borrowed money from her. The phrase is [zh:跟我借钱], 'borrowed money from me'. The reason? [zh:她妈妈生气了], 'Her mom got angry', and [zh:不给她钱了], 'won't give her money anymore.' The particle [zh:了] at the end indicates a change of state.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 说到钱,我的同屋今天跟我借钱。她说她妈妈生气了,不给她钱了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Shuō dào qián, wǒ de tóngwū jīntiān gēn wǒ jièqián. Tā shuō tā māma shēngqì le, bù gěi tā qián le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Speaking of money, my roommate borrowed money from me today. She said her mom got angry and wouldn't give her any more money.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A phrase used to introduce a new topic that is related to what was just mentioned.
Literally 'same room', a direct and common word for roommate.
The meaning (borrow or lend) depends on the prepositions used with it, like '跟' or '给'.
Literally 'give birth to air/gas', a vivid way to say someone is angry.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '说到钱' as a transition phrase. Show an arrow moving from a 'money' icon to a 'roommate' icon.

segments.segment_title: Usage: 借钱 (to borrow vs. to lend)
segments.teaching_goal: To correctly use '借钱' for both borrowing and lending by using the correct prepositions.

narration.subtitle_en: The verb [zh:借钱] can be confusing as it means both 'to borrow' and 'to lend'. The key is the preposition you use. To say 'A borrows from B', the structure is 'A [zh:跟] B [zh:借钱]'. To say 'A lends to B', the structure is 'A [zh:借钱给] B'. So, [zh:我跟朋友借钱] means 'I borrow money from a friend.' But [zh:我借钱给朋友] means 'I lend money to a friend.' Getting the prepositions [zh:跟] and [zh:给] right is essential.

on_screen_text.focus_text: A 跟 B 借钱 / A 借钱给 B
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: A gēn B jièqián / A jièqián gěi B
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: A borrows from B / A lends to B
on_screen_text.notes: Correct: 我的同屋跟我借钱。(My roommate borrows from me.) Incorrect: 我的同屋借钱我。(Missing the preposition '跟')

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb whose direction (borrowing or lending) is determined by prepositions.
Used here to indicate the source of borrowing.
Used here to indicate the recipient of lending.

segments.visual_notes: Use arrows to show the direction of money flow. 'A ← B' for 'A 跟 B 借钱' and 'A → B' for 'A 借钱给 B'.

segments.teaching_goal: To recognize and use the simple question '为什么?'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a short but essential question: [zh:为什么]. It simply means 'Why?'. It's the most common way to ask for a reason or an explanation for something. In this context, the speaker is curious about why the roommate's mother is angry.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 为什么?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wèishéme?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Why?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The standard question word for asking for a reason.

segments.visual_notes: Display a large question mark next to the characters '为什么'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a detailed explanation involving spending habits and the '不是...就是...' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence explains the 'why'. First, her parents had already paid for her meal money, [zh:饭钱]. The verb [zh:交了] means 'paid'. But, she didn't use it for meals. The sentence uses the structure [zh:不是...就是...], which means 'if it's not A, then it's B,' to describe her predictable habits. She either orders takeout, [zh:叫外卖], or goes to restaurants with classmates, [zh:跟同学去饭馆儿].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 她父母把她这个学期的饭钱都交了,可是她不是叫外卖,就是跟同学去饭馆儿...
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tā fùmǔ bǎ tā zhège xuéqī de fànqián dōu jiāo le, kěshì tā búshì jiào wàimài, jiùshì gēn tóngxué qù fànguǎnr...
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Her parents already paid for her meals for the semester, but she either orders takeout or goes to restaurants with classmates...
on_screen_text.notes: 不是 A, 就是 B: if it's not A, it's B.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Money specifically for buying food.
Used for paying fees, tuition, or submitting homework.

highlight_words.translation: if not A, then B; either A or B
highlight_words.explanation_en: A pattern to describe a limited set of predictable behaviors or scenarios.
Literally 'call for outside delivery'.

segments.visual_notes: Show two branching paths from the roommate: one to a 'takeout' icon and one to a 'restaurant' icon, linked by the '不是...就是...' structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn vocabulary related to reckless spending and being in debt.

narration.subtitle_en: The explanation continues, describing more bad habits. The adverb [zh:乱] is used to mean 'recklessly' or 'randomly'. She [zh:乱买东西], 'buys things impulsively,' and [zh:乱花钱], 'spends money recklessly.' As a result, she [zh:欠了] a lot of money. [zh:欠] means 'to owe'. Who does she owe? The bank, [zh:银行], and credit card companies, [zh:信用卡公司]. This paints a clear picture of her financial irresponsibility.

on_screen_text.focus_text: ...还常常乱买东西,乱花钱,欠了银行和信用卡公司很多钱。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: ...hái chángcháng luàn mǎi dōngxi, luàn huā qián, qiàn le yínháng hé xìnyòngkǎ gōngsī hěn duō qián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: ...She also often buys things impulsively, spends money recklessly, and owes a lot of money to the bank and credit card companies.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb indicating an action is done without order or care.
The verb for being in debt to someone or an institution.
Literally 'silver house' or 'silver establishment'.
Literally 'credit card', a direct translation.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the word '乱' appearing before '买东西' and '花钱'. Then show money flowing out and an arrow pointing to '银行' and '信用卡' icons with a minus sign.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the sentence describing someone running out of money quickly.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence describes the friend's financial situation. It says: 'The pocket money her parents gave her last month, she spent it all in less than ten days, and then asked her mom for more, but it still wasn't enough.' Let's break it down. [zh:零用钱] means 'pocket money' or 'allowance'. The phrase [zh:不到十天] means 'in less than ten days'. [zh:花完了] means 'spent it all,' with [zh:完] indicating completion. Finally, [zh:还是不够] means 'it still wasn't enough'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 上个月父母给的零用钱,她不到十天就花完了,又跟妈妈要了一些,还是不够。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Shàng ge yuè fùmǔ gěi de língyòngqián, tā bú dào shí tiān jiù huā wán le, yòu gēn māma yào le yìxiē, háishì bú gòu.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The pocket money her parents gave her last month, she spent it all in less than ten days, and then asked her mom for more, but it still wasn't enough.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to use '不到' to mean 'less than' a certain number or amount.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at [zh:不到]. It's a very useful phrase that means 'less than' or 'not yet reached' a certain number. In the dialogue, [zh:不到十天] means 'in less than ten days'. You can use it with time, age, or quantity. For example, [zh:认识不到一个月] means 'to have known each other for less than a month'. Or, for price, [zh:机票不到一千元] means 'the plane ticket costs less than one thousand yuan'.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: bú dào
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: less than; not yet
on_screen_text.notes:
Examples:
不到十天 (less than 10 days)
不到一个月 (less than a month)
不到六点 (not yet 6 o'clock)

highlight_words.explanation_en: Means 'has not reached a certain number' or 'less than.' From Language Note LN-7.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the expression of empathy for the parents and the rhetorical question.

narration.subtitle_en: This line expresses understanding for the mother's anger. It says: 'No wonder her mom is angry. Why doesn't she consider how hard it is for her parents to earn money!' The sentence starts with [zh:难怪], which means 'no wonder'. Then, [zh:她怎么不想想] is a rhetorical question, meaning 'Why doesn't she think about...'. The final part, [zh:父母挣钱多不容易啊], is an exclamation. It emphasizes how difficult it is for parents to earn money. The word [zh:多] here acts as an intensifier, like 'so' or 'how'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 难怪她妈妈生气。她怎么不想想,父母挣钱多不容易啊!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nánguài tā māma shēngqì. Tā zěnme bù xiǎngxiang, fùmǔ zhèngqián duō bù róngyì a!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: No wonder her mom is angry. Why doesn't she consider how hard it is for her parents to earn money!

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb used as an interjection, often at the beginning of a sentence, to express that something is understandable.
Literally 'to give birth to air/gas', a vivid way to describe being angry.
A common term for earning money through work.
An exclamatory pattern where '多' is used as an intensifier.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to use '难怪' to express that something is understandable.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on [zh:难怪]. It literally means 'hard to blame' and is used to mean 'no wonder'. You use it when you learn a piece of information that explains a situation. For example: 'He didn't study at all.' The logical conclusion? [zh:难怪他考试考得不好] - 'No wonder he did poorly on the test.' It's an interjection that often starts a sentence, showing a moment of realization.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 难怪
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nánguài
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: no wonder
on_screen_text.notes:
Used to express that a situation is now understood.
Example: 难怪他不高兴，原来他女朋友跟他分手了。
(No wonder he's unhappy, turns out his girlfriend broke up with him.)

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb used as an interjection, often at the beginning of a sentence. From Language Note LN-16.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to use '多...啊' for exclamations.

narration.subtitle_en: In the sentence, we saw [zh:多不容易啊]. Here, [zh:多] is not asking 'how many'. It's an intensifier used in exclamations to mean 'how' or 'so'. It expresses a strong feeling. For example, if it's very cold outside, you can exclaim, [zh:外边多冷啊]! meaning 'It's so cold outside!'. Or if you see something ugly, you might say [zh:多难看啊]! which means 'How ugly!'. It adds a lot of emotion to your statement.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: duō + Adj. + a/ya
on_screen_text.notes:
An exclamatory pattern to express strong emotion.
Example: 父母挣钱多不容易啊! (How difficult it is for parents to earn money!)

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can be used as an intensifier in exclamatory sentences. From Language Note LN-17.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the sentence agreeing with the previous speaker and stating a general principle.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a simple sentence of agreement. It says: 'Yeah, we shouldn't spend money recklessly.' The phrase [zh:是啊] is a common way to agree with someone, similar to 'Yeah' or 'That's right'. Then we have [zh:我们不应该], which means 'we should not'. What shouldn't we do? [zh:乱花钱]. [zh:乱] means 'randomly' or 'recklessly', and [zh:花钱] is 'to spend money'. So, [zh:乱花钱] means to spend money without a second thought.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 是啊,我们不应该乱花钱。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Shì a, wǒmen bù yīnggāi luàn huāqián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Yeah, we shouldn't spend money recklessly.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A particle phrase used to express agreement.
A modal verb indicating obligation or advisability.
'乱' means chaotic or reckless, '花钱' means to spend money.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the teasing tone, the rhetorical question, and the closing remarks.

narration.subtitle_en: This line has a slightly teasing tone. It says: 'Everyone knows you're your parents' good kid! Alright, you take your time looking for a job. We're leaving.' The first part, [zh:谁不知道], is another rhetorical question. It literally means 'Who doesn't know?', but it really means 'Everyone knows'. A [zh:乖孩子] is a 'good kid' or a 'well-behaved child'. The speaker then says [zh:好吧], 'Alright', and tells him [zh:你慢慢儿找工作], 'You take your time looking for a job'. Finally, [zh:我们走了] means 'We're leaving'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 谁不知道你是父母的乖孩子!好吧,你慢慢儿找工作,我们走了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Shéi bù zhīdào nǐ shì fùmǔ de guāi háizi! Hǎo ba, nǐ mànmanr zhǎo gōngzuò, wǒmen zǒu le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Everyone knows you're your parents' good kid! Alright, you take your time looking for a job. We're leaving.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A rhetorical question that means 'everyone knows'.
'乖' means well-behaved or obedient.
The reduplication of '慢' (slow) plus the '儿' suffix softens the tone and means 'unhurriedly'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the abstract, resultative meanings of the directional complements '下来' (xiàlái) and '出来' (chūlái).

narration.subtitle_en: Let's explore directional complements. While they often indicate literal direction, they can also express a result or state of being. First, let's look at [zh:下来]. It can imply the separation of one thing from another, or the completion of an action from start to finish. In the text, [zh:一生下来] means 'as soon as they were born,' marking the completion of birth. Next, we have [zh:出来]. This complement signifies emergence, like something coming out from a hidden place, or a change from having nothing to having something. For example, [zh:想出来一个好名字] means to 'come up with a good name,' as if pulling it out of your thoughts. Similarly, [zh:看出来] means 'to be able to tell' or 'to figure out by looking,' making something hidden now visible.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 孩子一生下来就开始存教育费。 / 我看出来他生气了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: háizi yī shēng xiàlái jiù kāishǐ cún jiàoyù fèi. / wǒ kàn chūlái tā shēngqì le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: As soon as the child is born, they start saving for education fees. / I could tell that he got angry.
on_screen_text.notes: Directional complements can express abstract results, not just physical direction.

grammar_points.explanation_en: Indicates detachment, separation, or the completion of a process from beginning to end.
Indicates emergence from hidden to visible, or from non-existence to existence.

segments.visual_notes: Animate an arrow moving 'down' for '下来' to show completion. Animate a lightbulb appearing for '出来' to show emergence of an idea.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use '来' to connect an action with its purpose, similar to 'in order to'.

narration.subtitle_en: Now let's look at a useful way to connect two actions. The word [zh:来] can be placed between two verb phrases to show that the first action is done for the purpose of the second. The structure is 'Verb Phrase 1 + [zh:来] + Verb Phrase 2'. In the dialogue, Tianming says he wants to [zh:找工作挣钱来减轻父母的负担]. Here, 'finding a job and earning money' is the action, and 'lessening his parents' burden' is the purpose. Another example could be: [zh:大家计划开一个舞会来给他过生日], 'Everyone planned a dance party in order to celebrate his birthday.' It's a great way to make your sentences more descriptive and show clear intent.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 找工作挣钱来减轻父母的负担。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zhǎo gōngzuò zhèng qián lái jiǎnqīng fùmǔ de fùdān.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: to find a job and earn money in order to lessen the parents' burden.

grammar_points.pattern: 动词短语1 + 来 + 动词短语2
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: dòngcí duǎnyǔ yī lái dòngcí duǎnyǔ èr
grammar_points.explanation_en: Connects an action (VP1) with its purpose (VP2), meaning 'in order to'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand that '了' is often omitted for completed actions that serve as background information for the main narrative.

narration.subtitle_en: You've learned that the particle [zh:了] marks a completed action. But sometimes, it's missing even when an action is in the past. Why? It depends on whether the action is part of the main story or just background information. In our text, the sentence [zh:孩子一生下来就开始给他们存教育费] describes a past, completed action, but it serves as background context. Therefore, there's no [zh:了] after [zh:存]. However, when the main narrative begins with [zh:这时候丽莎和雪梅走了进来], the verb [zh:走] is followed by [zh:了] because it's a key event happening in the story's 'present'. So, think of it this way: background details often omit [zh:了], while main narrative actions include it.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 背景: ...开始给他们存教育费。(No 了) / 主要叙述: 这时候丽莎和雪梅走了进来。(Has 了)
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Bèijǐng: ...kāishǐ gěi tāmen cún jiàoyù fèi. / Zhǔyào xùshù: Zhè shíhou Lìshā hé Xuěméi zǒu le jìnlái.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Background: ...started saving for their education fees. / Main Narrative: At this moment, Lisha and Xuemei walked in.
on_screen_text.notes: Use '了' for key actions in the main story. Omit '了' for background information.

grammar_points.explanation_en: When a completed action provides context or background, '了' is often omitted.
When a completed action is a key event in the main storyline, '了' is used.

segments.visual_notes: Show the 'background' sentence in a faded or smaller font, and the 'main narrative' sentence in a bold, prominent font to visually distinguish them.

segments.teaching_goal: To recognize and understand rhetorical questions, which are used to make a strong point rather than to ask for an answer.

narration.subtitle_en: Sometimes, a question isn't really a question. We call these rhetorical questions. They are used to make a point more emphatically. A negative question often implies a strong positive meaning. For example, when Lisha says [zh:谁不知道你是父母的乖孩子], she's not asking 'Who doesn't know?'. She's stating, 'Everyone knows you're a good kid!' Conversely, an affirmative question can express a strong negative or a complaint. When Tianming says [zh:她怎么不想想], he's not genuinely asking why. He's making a strong statement: 'She really should think about how hard it is for her parents to earn money!' So, listen for the tone and context to spot these.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 谁不知道你是父母的乖孩子！ (Meaning: 大家都知道...)
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Q: Shéi bù zhīdào nǐ shì fùmǔ de guāi háizi! (Meaning: Dàjiā dōu zhīdào...)
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: Who doesn't know you're your parents' good kid! (Meaning: Everyone knows...)
on_screen_text.notes: Rhetorical questions use a question format to make a strong statement.

grammar_points.explanation_en: Example: '谁不知道...' (Who doesn't know...) means 'Everyone knows...'
Example: '她怎么不...' (Why doesn't she...) means 'She really should...'

segments.visual_notes: Animate a question mark turning into an exclamation point to illustrate the concept.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Die Lektion beginnt mit der Vorstellung von Zhang Tianmings familiärem Hintergrund. Dieser erste Satzteil besagt, dass seine Eltern hoffen, ihre Kinder könnten eine gute Ausbildung erhalten. Das Verb [zh:受到] bedeutet „erhalten“ oder „bekommen“. Es wird oft mit abstrakten Nomen wie „Bildung“ verwendet. [zh:良好] ist eine formellere Art, „gut“ oder „fein“ auszudrücken. [zh:受到良好的教育] bedeutet also „eine gute Ausbildung erhalten“.

segments.teaching_goal: Verstehe den zweiten Teil des Satzes über das Sparen von Geld und lerne das Verb „cún“ [zh:存].

narration_track.subtitle_en: Dieser Satzteil erklärt das Ergebnis ihrer Hoffnungen. Es heißt: „Sie begannen also, für den Bildungsfonds ihrer Kinder zu sparen, sobald diese geboren waren.“ Die Schlüsselphrase hier ist [zh:一生下来]. Sie bedeutet „sobald man geboren ist“. Das Verb [zh:存] bedeutet „sparen“ oder „einzahlen“. Das ist wie Geld auf einer Bank einzahlen. [zh:存教育费] bedeutet also, für die Ausbildungskosten zu sparen.

content.focus_text: 所以孩子一生下来就开始给他们存教育费。

Dieser erste Teil stellt fest, dass die Familie nicht arm ist.

segments.teaching_goal: Verstehe das Konzept des finanziellen Drucks und lerne die Wörter „gōng“ [zh:供] und „yālì“ [zh:压力].

narration_track.subtitle_en: Hier ist der Kontrast. Es beginnt mit [zh:但是], was „aber“ bedeutet. Es erklärt, dass die Unterstützung von Tianming und seiner Schwester während des Studiums immer noch finanziellen Druck erzeugt. Das Verb [zh:供] bedeutet hier „finanziell unterstützen“. Das Schlüsselwort ist [zh:压力], das „Druck“ bedeutet. [zh:有压力] bedeutet also „Druck haben“ oder „unter Druck stehen“.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Dieser Satz erklärt Tianmings Sichtweise. Er fühlt sich bereits als [zh:大人], also als „Erwachsener“. Deshalb sollte er einen Job finden, um die Last seiner Eltern zu mindern. Das Verb [zh:减轻] bedeutet „mindern“ oder „erleichtern“. Es wird oft mit abstrakten Nomen wie „Last“ oder „Druck“ verwendet. Das Wort für Last ist [zh:负担]. Die Partikel [zh:来] verbindet die beiden Handlungen. Sie zeigt, dass „Geld verdienen“ das Mittel ist, um die Last zu mindern.
